artifacts.content:
Hi Javi,

Kids are finally in bed and Daniel’s on the late shift at La Casa de Miguel, so I’m catching up on life with you instead of grading IEPs.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how the last few years piled up on us. Remember January 2022 when Dr. Patel from UW Health East Clinic called me during the school day about Sofia’s chest X-ray? I was in my resource room at Jefferson, and when she said there was post-COVID lung inflammation I just lost it. Karen Lewis, our school nurse, ended up calling 911 and they took me by ambulance to the UW Hospital ER. They labeled it a panic attack and referred me to weekly telehealth counseling. The therapy helped, but even the $45 co-pay each time added up. I’ve basically stopped going since last October because I just can’t keep adding to the bills.

Then there’s the older stuff that hasn’t gone away. When Daniel’s restaurant shut down for those three weeks back in March 2021, I took that $2,000 cash advance on the Capital One card to keep us afloat after we missed April rent at The Pines on Schroeder. Mark Jensen filed that 5-day notice and started the eviction case in Dane County small claims. If Mami hadn’t wired the $600 on March 29, I honestly don’t know where we’d be living right now. We’re still paying off the court fees and 24.9% interest from that whole mess.

Day to day, life is okay—Lucas has soccer twice this week, Sofia has a pulmonology check next month, and my students are in full spring meltdown mode before testing. I’m trying to cook more beans and rice and fewer frozen pizzas, and I keep telling myself this is just a season. But sometimes it feels like we’re one broken timing belt away from everything crashing again.

Love you. Thanks for always picking up the phone when I spin out.

-M

metadata.subject: Catching you up (for real this time)

artifacts.content:
Personal reflection – late night money/mental health brain dump

It’s almost midnight and I should be finishing progress notes, but my mind keeps looping back to that day in January 2022 in my resource room at Jefferson. Dr. Patel called from UW Health East Clinic about Sofia’s chest X-ray showing post-COVID lung inflammation, and I went straight into panic mode. Karen Lewis had to call 911, and I ended up in the UW Hospital ER on 1/19/22. They set me up with weekly telehealth counseling afterward, which was actually helpful, but I’ve been skipping sessions since October 2023 because I can’t keep paying the $45 co-pay on top of everything else.

Then there’s October 2022, meeting with Michael Chen at his little office on Odana Road about possibly filing Chapter 13. He quoted $3,500 plus court costs and warned that if we’re ever more than 60 days behind on the 2013 Honda CR-V again, the lender can repossess it. Even the $150 consultation fee went straight onto a credit card. It was sobering realizing how close we are to losing the car I need for my commute and Sofia’s appointments.

I keep beating myself up because if I’d been more on top of budgeting earlier in life, maybe we’d have some cushion. I’ve never been great at planning ahead with money; I tend to just pay whatever bill is screaming the loudest and hope the rest works out. Not exactly a system.

Tomorrow: call UW billing again about the ER plan, double-check the CR-V payment posted, and try to schedule at least one therapy session next month, even if it means cutting something else.

Thread with: Mariah (6th grade ELA)

Melissa: You still up? I’m staring at this pile of IEP drafts and my brain is mush.

Mariah: Same. Progress report season is the worst. What’s going on?

Melissa: Just thinking how different things might be if I’d made some better choices when everything shut down.

Mariah: Girl, we were all in survival mode.

Melissa: Remember spring 2020 when we closed buildings? I was so overwhelmed trying to learn Zoom, Canvas, Google everything, plus juggling Lucas and Sofia’s remote classes. They offered those extra virtual tutoring hours, and I said no because I felt like I was drowning in tech. Now I keep kicking myself—those hours would’ve helped so much when Daniel’s restaurant closed and we started falling behind.

Mariah: You were doing the best you could. And you were still running IEP meetings from your classroom half the time.

Melissa: Yeah, that’s the other thing. Between April and June 2020 I sometimes left the kids home for a couple hours to go into Jefferson to pick up special ed packets and run IEP Zooms from my room. One day in May, our neighbor Carla in 3B heard Sofia coughing on the balcony and called the non-emergency line. CPS worker Angela Brooks showed up and opened a short-term case. It closed in August 2020, but I still get a pit in my stomach thinking about it.

Mariah: Oh my god, I had no idea. That’s terrifying.

Melissa: It made me even more scared to take on anything extra. Now we’re still climbing out of the hole.

Mariah: Be kinder to yourself. Seriously. Also, bring coffee tomorrow. I’ll trade you grading time during my prep.

Hey Ana,

I owe you a real update instead of the half-sent voice notes between bus duty and bedtime.

Work is…a lot. During the Omicron surge back in January 2022, I had that awful panic attack in my resource room at Jefferson. Dr. Patel from UW Health East Clinic called about Sofia’s chest X-ray showing post-COVID lung inflammation, and I just fell apart. Karen, our school nurse, called 911, and I ended up at the UW Hospital ER on 1/19/22. They put me on weekly telehealth counseling, which honestly kept me functioning, but I’ve had to cut way back since last fall because the $45 co-pays keep piling up.

Fast forward a bit: in February 2022, when I actually caught COVID myself, my principal, Karen Schultz, pulled me aside (virtually, of course) and gently suggested I consider FMLA or unpaid leave. The brain fog was so bad I missed a couple of virtual IEP deadlines. Then on March 4, 2022, she added a little note in my evaluation about “timeliness of documentation.” I get that she had to, but it felt like a punch when we were already losing income from my unpaid sick days and Daniel’s hours being cut again at La Casa de Miguel.

On top of that, we’re still dragging around old debt—remember that big family trip we put on the card in early 2019? At the time it felt worth it, but we’re unbelievably still paying chunks of it off, which makes every new emergency feel heavier.

Day-to-day stuff: Lucas made the rec soccer team again, Sofia’s obsessed with Dog Man books, and I’m trying to meal plan so we’re not ordering takeout when I’m wiped. Friday is still family movie night when Daniel isn’t on a double.

If you ever feel like visiting Madison, I have a couch, extra blankets, and a lot of tea.

Love,
Melissa

Event: Bankruptcy consult – Michael Chen
Date: Tuesday, October 11, 2022
Time: 4:00–5:00 p.m.
Location: Michael Chen Law Office, Odana Road
Attendees: Melissa Rodriguez

Notes:

Leave Jefferson right at contract time (3:45) and drive straight to Odana. Bring:
- Folder with UW Hospital ER bill from 1/19/22 (panic attack after Dr. Patel’s call about Sofia’s post-COVID lung inflammation in my resource room)
- Statements for Sofia’s pulmonology visits and the telehealth counseling sessions (those $45 co-pays)
- Capital One statement showing the $2,000 cash advance from March 2021 when La Casa de Miguel shut down for three weeks and we missed April rent at The Pines on Schroeder. Include record of the 5-day notice and Dane County small claims eviction case Mark Jensen filed before Mom wired $600 on 3/29/21.
- Current car loan info for the 2013 Honda CR-V.

Questions for Michael Chen:
1. Realistic to do Chapter 13 with our income? He mentioned $3,500 fee plus court costs on the phone—need to understand monthly payment.
2. How serious is the risk of repossession if we ever go 60+ days behind on the CR-V again?
3. Any impact on teaching license renewal or future apartment applications?

Reminder: consultation fee is $150—put on debit card if possible, only use credit if absolutely necessary.

metadata.title: Bankruptcy consult – Michael Chen
metadata.location: Michael Chen Law Office, Odana Road

artifacts.content:
Weekly planning – kids, work, money

1. Kids
- Lucas: soccer practice Tues/Thurs 5:30–7:00 at Olbrich field. Pack snacks so we’re not tempted by drive-thru. Bring a book and maybe some IEP progress notes to the sidelines.
- Sofia: asthma follow-up with Dr. Patel at UW Health East Clinic next month—call to confirm date/time, make sure inhalers at school and in backpack are not expired.

2. Work
- Finish progress notes for Jefferson Middle IEPs by Thursday.
- Follow up with Karen Schultz about that “timeliness of documentation” comment from 3/4/22 eval—ask what she needs to see this spring.
- Check in with Karen Lewis about updated emergency plan for if I have another panic episode at school (still embarrassed about the 911 call from my resource room in Jan 2022, but better to have a plan).

3. Finances
- Call UW Hospital billing about ER visit from 1/19/22 and telehealth therapy charges—see if they’ll extend the payment plan so I can schedule at least one counseling session next month without feeling guilty about the $45 co-pay.
- Double-check auto-pay for the 2013 Honda CR-V so we don’t even get close to that 60+ days behind mark Michael Chen warned about.
- Review gas/electric bill; try to trim usage (shorter showers, full loads of laundry only).

4. Self
- One walk around the neighborhood after dinner at least 3 nights this week.
- Read a chapter of a non-teaching book before bed instead of doom-scrolling.

Mami,

Solo tengo un ratito before I start dinner, but I wanted to write instead of just sending you pictures.

We’re okay, just tired. Daniel’s hours at La Casa de Miguel are a little better, but still not like before COVID. I’m still at Jefferson Middle in special ed, nine years now. Some days I feel strong, other days I feel like I’m made of glass.

Do you remember when Daniel’s restaurant closed for three weeks in March 2021 because of the COVID outbreak? That was when we missed April rent at The Pines on Schroeder. The landlord, Mark Jensen, gave us that 5-day notice and even started an eviction case in Dane County. I still get shaky thinking about it. If you hadn’t wired the $600 on March 29 so we could make a partial payment and set up a plan, I honestly think we would have been out on the street.

Then in February 2022 I got COVID myself. The brain fog was so bad I missed a couple of virtual IEP deadlines. My principal, Dr. Karen Schultz, suggested maybe I should go on FMLA or unpaid leave. I didn’t, because we couldn’t survive with even less money, but she still put a note in my March 4, 2022 evaluation about “timeliness of documentation.” I’m scared it makes me look unreliable, even though I was really sick.

On top of all that, I met with a bankruptcy lawyer, Michael Chen, on Odana Road in October 2022. He said Chapter 13 would cost around $3,500 and warned that if we ever get more than 60 days behind again on the 2013 Honda CR-V, they can repossess it. Even his $150 consultation had to go on a card.

But listen—Lucas is reading you-know-who Harry Potter in Spanish now, and Sofia drew a picture of you with a big crown like a reina. We’re coming to Milwaukee two Saturdays from now to help with groceries and laundry. I’ll call you tomorrow night after bedtime.

Te quiero mucho,
Melissa
